speaker: interviewer
text: Betsy [? Stern ?] interviewing Janet Rogowsky-- May 3, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Am I ready to go on?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did we finish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were telling us about what happened after your husband died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, I'm-- yes, and I'm talking about when I was working in another place. Later on I was working in a-- in a brassiere factory in the office. So also there were three brothers-- both of them very highly educated. Very, very nice and treated me very nice. And every year-- every week they were giving me a raise. And-- and I was good at this work. And when [? Feliks ?] died I decided to go to-- back to school. So they were very upset. They offered me stocks in the firm. They offered me a big raise, and they said you'll never make money teaching as much as I will offer you. And I said, but it's not a question of money. I interrupted my education because I wanted my husband to become very successful, and then I felt I can go back to school. But since this happened, I want to go now. So-- and I thought I made a very good decision. And I got into Columbia. Oh, first I went to Brooklyn College, and then I went to Columbia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide you wanted to teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I really-- my sister-in-law, who is a dentist, she really wanted me to go on with medical school. I was very reluctant because, first of all, I didn't have the money. And I didn't expect my family to support me. And they wouldn't have, and I wouldn't blame them. And I worked very hard in Brooklyn College, because besides carrying 19 credits, I also worked. And I was in my student teaching, so I really was absolutely exhausted. And I finished in two and a half years. Because I had two sessions in the summer also, so it was 12 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was-- and medical school would have been a lot of work and I couldn't have worked. But she insisted I should apply. We'll see what happens. And I was very nice to her when she was in need. But she-- at that time, she was still struggling financially too because she had to go back to school herself. So I did apply to Switzerland. And I was accepted to-- in Zurich. By then, when the acceptance came, and I felt, even if I don't need as much as in the United States, I certainly will need a few thousand dollars a year. And it's impossible for me, and I absolutely refuse to ask anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you, Betsy, that was the best choice that I made. I love teaching. I was lucky. First of all, I never had any other interview except Mamaroneck, Scarsdale, and Great Neck. Those were the three schools Columbia suggested. Each school wanted me. Scarsdale wanted me-- offered me $400 more. Of course at that time it started, what, with $3,000, $3,500. And they told me here that Scarsdale would pay me more. But I liked this school because of the diversity of kids, and they were a little bit too stiff in Great Neck and in Scarsdale for me. And I felt I can do something with young people. And the orphanage proved that I could do it-- miracles, practically. I had the best years of my life. It was wonderful. It's a very good community. The community was delighted. I was probably the second Jewish teacher here. They were not hiring Jews here. And as a matter of fact, I couldn't even get-- I thought I will never be able to get an apartment. I was the second Jewish person in-- in the project in an apartment house here. They didn't-- they rented to Jews. And I was upset with my colleague who was Jewish and said that he had to promise when he was hired a few years earlier that he will not admit that he was Jewish. I said, I lose all my respect for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a matter of fact, when I came to teach and came to the holidays, even if I was not a member of the synagogue, I made an announcement that tomorrow's a Jewish holiday, and I will not be here. And I realized later that the Jewish kids-- in the beginning, I didn't know it. In the beginning I asked, how many of you are going to be absent tomorrow? Let's say, it was Sukkot or something like that. And nobody would raise their hands. And I said to myself, I don't have any Jewish kids here. But then they, one by one, they came over to me and said, Mrs. Rogowsky, I will not be here tomorrow because it's a Jewish holiday. I said, why didn't you tell me that when you were in class? And they would shrug with the shoulder. So I told them later on that I don't think you're right. You're wrong. I don't think you're right. I said, if people will dislike you because you're Jewish, you don't want them. And I think you should not be afraid to say it and not be ashamed of it. So anytime there was a holiday, Yom Kippur, if I was absent, I would always tell them. I will not be here tomorrow because of a Jewish holiday. And fine, I'll tell you. I didn't have any problems. And I had a lot of very nice non-Jewish friends here and Jews. And I was very happy in teaching. Did you tell people about your war time experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in a minute I'll tell you. And I just want to finish. And I'll tell you something else, why I liked it so much. That gave me a lot of room for grow-- growing because I had a long summer. I would be either in Europe or in another, I would get a scholarship to another university in California or Colorado. I would study a lot, I would go on to Columbia. And I traveled a lot, even while-- even while I was still single without David. And the kids taught me a lot. And I-- I thought it was a terrific profession for me. It was a wonderful profession. I was offered the positions in college. I didn't want to do it. I-- I thought it was terrific here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: I taught French and Russian. What-- what was your question? I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you tell people about-- about your wartime experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it is strange. In the beginning-- of course, I didn't talk too much-- but then I started to talk. And do you know that some parents, in a very nice way, called me up and said, do me a favor. Don't-- my daughter didn't sleep all night. She-- she was scared. Somebody called up, do me a favor, my son couldn't take it. In a very nice way, some people called me and thanked me for the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I realized that I have a problem, that some wanted me to do it. So I called-- I talked to my students outside of school, not in class. Once I got very upset when one of my students went already to Brown and came back. And she said, you know, I went to purchase to a lecture. And I-- and somebody spoke about the Holocaust, and I was very upset that I knew so little about it. I said, and you mean to tell me that you didn't learn enough from me, after all I taught? She said, Mrs. Rogowsky, not that much. And I was very upset with myself that-- that some kids really wanted to hear more, but, you know, I couldn't ignore the others. So I-- I-- evidently they felt that I should have spoken a little bit more to them. What about other people? Not-- not your students, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I must tell you, we retired seven years ago. Probably during this period, David learned more about me than throughout the marriage, than in the previous 16 years. Because, number one, I didn't think it's fair to him. I-- I think that no matter-- no matter how much you want to hear, I mean, you don't-- people don't want to hear all the time about-- Even from your own wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you think that, that people don't want to hear all the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'll tell you. You ask my sister-in-law a question, she gets adamant. She refuses even to look at a film. She refuses-- when I told her I'm having this today, somehow trying to ask, she right away changed the subject. Good for you, and she went on. She gets angry. If-- I tell you, if I ask her something about my brother, that I wanted to write about my brother, she-- she-- she gets hysterical. She absolutely doesn't want to hear about the-- about the past. She completely-- completely locks herself up. Her sister, who was interviewed, she-- there was a time that she wanted to go to the movies. And we talked a lot it, how I feel. And she's doing much, much better. But still, there's certain films she will not see. I sometimes am tortured, and I will see it. And I feel in the long run I'm better off-- because you do get it in the beginning, of course, when you see a film, you get upset and you might not sleep a night or two. But you get it out of your system. And I think it's good. But a lot of people, a lot of people, will not talk about the past. I have a friend who was interviewed already in Jersey. She is driving me crazy. As soon as I see her, David also, as soon as we come there, the first thing she'll talk about is the past. And sometimes I'll say, let's not do it. We can't each time talk about it. But some people have a great urge. But the majority of my friends who survived will not talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about people, who didn't-- Americans, who want to hear? What was their reaction to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- Most Americans that I know, my family made a big mistake. The first night they told me, they said they were up with me all night when they picked me up. And they heard the whole whatever I had to tell them. And then she said, and let's not talk about it. And they really did not want to hear one single word. That's the reason I didn't know names. They had brothers and sisters. They absolutely refused to talk about it. It was done, it's gone, and this is it. A lot of people that I dealt with did not want to hear about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that make you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is-- I'll tell you, at one time I was very hurt, because first of all, they like shut me up. And I needed it. And-- but when the war-- the Vietnam War came, and when people-- or [INAUDIBLE] the Korean War, when people came and talked about-- about the war, and I was sitting and eating a steak at the table, I said to myself, you know, it's true I keep saying, oy, and it's bad, and people are killed-- but even in Bosnia now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, you say-- you can-- you say to yourself, what do you do to help these people? And I do nothing except saying, too bad, or I would give a donation. And so I said to myself, I can now understand my family, why they didn't want me to talk, because they don't want to hear about it. Because they-- as far as they are concerned, everybody is gone, they can't do anything, and let's go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can ask me a question, how do I feel about German students, about German friends. I have an Austrian friend who tells me that her family was the most anti-Semitic family that you can encounter. She's honest about it. But my argument is, you can't go on forever blaming a whole nation. There was some Germans who were good too. It's terrible to admit, but it's true. There's some Poles that were good. Some-- Many Poles saved lives. Many Germans saved lives. So you can't-- so you have to somehow become a little philosophical too. And, of course, it-- it was a terrible disaster. And the more you read about it, you-- you realize what-- what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But sometimes I-- people ask me a question, what would you do if someone would say, this guy killed your father? Could you kill him? And I couldn't. The only thing, my biggest satisfaction probably would be to spit in his face and hope that he suffers for the rest of his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I don't have any-- it's-- it's-- I don't know what it is, but I have no vengeance. I just feel that it was a mad world. And I hate to say that. I'm afraid it might happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess this is a good time to ask you, and maybe that's what you've been telling us anyway, what you would like people to learn from your experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, you have to teach people that there is still a lot of goodness in this world. There's still a lot of good people. And the fact that this kind of disaster took place does not mean that the whole world is bad. I-- I would rather-- and I'm going now to listen to a lecture on Wednesday that Professor [? Goldhagen, ?] who just came out with a book, if you read about it, is-- is lecturing in our community on Wednesday. And I-- I don't believe that all Germans, as he feels, that it's not only Hitler's-- all Germans were bad and helped. It is true that a lot of Germans helped. But I really want to defend them, in a way, and say, if you indoctrinate people from the very early age when they're five years old and keep telling them, you are the best and you-- you-- you are the most popular, you are the most beautiful, and people there are evil and they want to take it away from you. And keep repeating after me, this is no good, and go on. And they become-- age 18, 19 taken to the army, or whatever it is, or to the Party and then they tell you to kill, they do it because they really were indoctrinated. But-- and it was a mad world. I-- a lot of Germans were very, very bad. But there are a lot of Germans who risked their life and-- and hid Jewish girls, whether they could have been killed any moment. And I know those girls who survived, and they told me those stories.

words.text: or to the Party and then they

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can't go on forever and-- and have anger. You have to-- or be-- or want revenge. And I feel that as far as I'm concerned, it's-- maybe you have to go through a disaster, through such a tragedy as I did in my family, to understand somehow the mentality of people and to learn about forgiveness, and to become a little bit more philosophical about life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe that's why I'm more understanding, let's say, about our own problems in this country now. I'm very-- I'm heartbroken with what I hear and see. I know I-- when I was in Paris once for vacation I saw a TV program from the United States castrating a black in the South. And this problem was never shown here in this country. And I was heartbroken when I came to this country, and I went to washing room and I saw two fountains, water fountains-- one was for black-- on the street-- and one for whites. I went to Florida. I was sitting in a bus traveling in Miami. And a black woman, an older woman, came in and wanted to-- and I got up to give her the seat. Everybody started to look at me. I had no idea why. And she went all the way to the back. Later I realized that she had no right to sit there. To me, that was-- I went with students in Oregon, and we went for a beer. And people didn't want to serve it-- those were teachers, not student teachers-- because there were two black teachers among us. They didn't want to serve us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to me this, after going through, I'm not saying that only those who went through a Holocaust become a little bit more understanding. I'm sure there are a lot of Americans that are more liberal than I am. But you do change a lot. And you do-- you do develop different values.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even in marriage, it helped me a lot because I-- I believe that you can-- you can have a very good marriage if you're not petty, if you are not-- if you're more understanding, if-- if you understand somebody else's problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's why maybe I was a better teacher because I wanted to-- kids to understand-- I was talking in general with them a lot. In my class, very often I would-- when take them-- took a look after a half an hour, and I saw some kids were falling asleep, I closed the book. And I said, what-- what are we talking about today? What is the topic?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had nothing to do even with French and Russian, to-- to give them a boost. And they came up with all kinds of problems, about love and parents and what. And we had a terrific relationship. And any time they had problems, they would come, and we'd sit down and we'd talk together. And I think I had a lot of respect for them and they had a lot of respect for me. So that helped me also. So I think that some-- some good came out of that. Of course, there was an awful lot of tragedy that came out, and I don't wish it on anybody. But I think I-- I came out all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure. I want you to meet David, my husband. We are married over 34 years and very happily married. And this is the best thing that happened to me in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's actually been 33 years. It only seems like 34.

speaker: interviewee
text: July.

speaker: interviewee
text: July. But they've been terrific years. And I must say that I-- I learned a lot during this time. One of the first things I-- I realized when Jan and I first started going together-- she never really talked much about her experiences, but I knew that she had survived the Holocaust. It was the role that fate plays in life. While she was struggling for survival in her teenage years, here I was in a suburban high school. And my biggest worry was whether I was going to make the basketball team or not. And the-- the only deprivation was because there was a shortage of gasoline during the war, they didn't let me drive until I was 18. But over the years as I learned more about Janet, she taught me a great deal. One of most important things she taught me really was to appreciate every day that you're alive. I have a tendency to procrastinate. And Janet always would say, you know, let's do it today-- you don't know what tomorrow would bring. And she certainly has been right about that. She also taught me that revenge and bitterness are two things that you-- you can't afford to have if you want to have a happy life. And no matter what her experience has been, she's always been forgiving, and she's never allowed a bitterness to-- to take over her life. She has a tremendous zest for living and a tremendous desire to learn. And she's put-- made that a part of my life. And I've always said that I've learned more with her and from her than from any other experience in my life. And I wouldn't trade our years together for anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a book about my hometown, Ostrog, Poland, and about the families that lived in Ostrog. In the upper left is a picture of my father, Shamai Ajenberg. Next to him is my mother, Miriam. In the lower left is my oldest brother, Abrasha Ajenberg. And to his right is my sister, Asya Ajenberg, married later to [? Stach ?] Siedlecka. Those are my paternal grandparents, Shaya Ajenberg, and his wife, Rifka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, It probably says on the other side. Should I-- can I look? This picture was taken in Ostrog, Poland around 1931, '32. To the left is my mother, Miriam. Next to her is Uncle Joe. Next to him is my Aunt [? Florence. ?] And next to her is my father, Shamai Ajenberg. This is my sister, Ania Ajenberg. The picture was taken before the war, probably around 1937 in Ostrog, Poland. This is my brother, Grisza Ajenberg, and his fiance. It was taken in [? Wilia, ?] Poland around 1937. This is of my brother, Abrasha Ajenberg. And it was taken in Ostrog, Poland. This picture is a graduation photo. It was taken probably around 1930. This is Mr. Nowotynsky, my father's lawyer, who helped us to survive the war. And it was taken before the war around 1935-36.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you come to have that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess he gave it to us. Gave it probably to-- probably to us, gave it to. This is a self-portrait of [? Feliks ?] Puterman. He was in Sachsenhausen concentration camp and he was 78 pounds at that time when this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that portrait now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Tel Aviv Museum. I donated it to the Tel Aviv Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was first your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? Feliks ?] Puterman. And the picture was taken in Warsaw in 1950. This is a picture of me, Janet Rogowsky. And it was taken in Warsaw in 1946. This is a memorial to those who perished in the Holocaust. And I think it's an old Jewish cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it is in Ostrog, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't touch. Don't touch.
